title: 15-Year-Old Female Adolescent Annual Well-Care Visit + Soccer Sports Physical Clearance with Confidential HEADSSS Interview Revealing Positive PHQ-9 Depression Screen (16/27 Moderately Severe) + Mild Passive Suicidal Ideation Without Plan or Intent: Safety Planning, Same-Day Mental Health Intake, ACIP Immunization Catch-Up, Confidentiality + Adolescent Consent Navigation, and Anticipatory Guidance
subtitle: ADOLESCENT WELL-CARE per Bright Futures + AAP: annual visit 11–21 yr with confidential HEADSSS interview (Home + Education/Employment + Activities + Drugs + Sexuality + Suicide/Mental Health + Safety); UPDATED expanded SSHADESS or HEEADSSS adds Eating Disorders + Spirituality; CONFIDENTIALITY framework — caregiver out of room for confidential portion (10+ min minimum); CA Family Code minor consent provisions allow 12+ to consent to mental health (FC §6924) + reproductive health/contraception (§6925) + STI testing/treatment (§6926) + substance use treatment (§6929) + sexual assault evaluation (§6927); EXCEPTIONS to confidentiality = mandated reporting (child abuse + neglect) + IMMINENT DANGER to self or others (suicide ideation with plan/intent/access; homicidal ideation) + court order; ACIP IMMUNIZATIONS due at this visit per catch-up = HPV (2-dose series if started <15 yr, 3-dose if started ≥15 yr; this patient received #1 + #2 at age 11 + 12 — series complete), Tdap (received age 11), MenACWY (received age 11; BOOSTER due at 16), MenB (shared clinical decision-making at 16–23 — discuss but not routine), influenza annually, COVID-19 per current guidance; SCREENINGS — PHQ-9 + Columbia/ASQ suicide + CRAFFT substance use + sexual health + eating disorder + intimate partner violence + concussion + safety + lead exposure history + family hx + Tanner staging done sensitively + BP + vision + hearing + dental + lipid screen once 9–11 yr + once 17–21 yr + HIV screen once 15–21 yr (and earlier if risk) + chlamydia + gonorrhea screen for sexually active females; SPORTS PHYSICAL clearance per PPE-5 standard (cardiac history including SCA family hx + concussion history + female athlete triad/RED-S + nutrition + ergogenic substances); POSITIVE PHQ-9 management — PHQ-9 ≥10 = positive; ≥15 moderately severe; ≥20 severe; THIS PATIENT 16/27 moderately severe; SUICIDE risk stratification — ideation (yes) + plan (no) + intent (no) + access to means (no specific firearms; will discuss with family) + protective factors (close grandmother + soccer + dog + future plans for college) = MODERATE risk; SAFETY PLAN (Stanley-Brown brief intervention) — warning signs + internal coping strategies + social distractions + people for support + professionals + safe environment + lethal means restriction (firearms locked + medications secured); SAME-DAY mental health intake activated; PARENT engagement WITH patient consent for safety info (age-appropriate boundary — share safety concerns + plan + lethal means counseling but not full HEADSSS content unless patient consents); ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE — sleep + nutrition + screen time + healthy relationships + consent + driving safety + college + identity + emerging autonomy; FOLLOW-UP — outpatient mental health 1 week + PCP follow-up 2 weeks + sports clearance pending after stability + family education + safety plan handoff

labs.v: BRIGHT FUTURES + AAP recommends annual well-care visits for adolescents 11–21 yr with adolescent-friendly approach. STRUCTURE: (1) initial joint portion with caregiver (~10 min) covering chief concerns + interval history + family history + current medications + immunization status + growth + general health; (2) CONFIDENTIAL portion with adolescent alone (~15–25 min) covering HEADSSS interview + screenings + sensitive topics + sexual health + mental health + substance use + safety; (3) physical exam (caregiver presence optional per patient preference + age — generally adolescent's choice + chaperone offered for sensitive exams); (4) joint summary + anticipatory guidance + plan + immunizations + referrals. CONFIDENTIALITY: every visit must explicitly explain confidentiality + its limits BEFORE the confidential portion begins. SCRIPT EXAMPLE: 'What you tell me is confidential — meaning I won't share it with your mom unless YOU give me permission OR you tell me you're in immediate danger of hurting yourself or others, OR someone is hurting you, OR I'm required by law to report (like if I learn of child abuse or neglect). Otherwise this is between you and me.' MINOR CONSENT in CALIFORNIA per Family Code: §6924 mental health services at age 12+ if clinician determines maturity + outpatient (not residential); §6925 contraception + pregnancy at any age; §6926 STI testing + treatment + HIV testing at age 12+; §6927 sexual assault evaluation + treatment at any age; §6928 prenatal care + delivery at any age; §6929 outpatient substance use treatment at age 12+. PARENT NOTIFICATION not required for minor-consented services. CONFIDENTIALITY also extends to PORTIONS of records (sensitive areas) per minor consent — billing may be separated from parent's view per institutional protocol. PROTECTING THE THERAPEUTIC ALLIANCE = the foundation of effective adolescent care + worth careful attention. EXCEPTIONS = mandated reporting (child abuse + neglect — RN is mandated reporter all 50 states + CA Penal Code §11164–11174.3 CANRA) + IMMINENT DANGER to self/others + court orders. ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE topics per age — adolescents = identity development + peer relationships + body changes + sexuality + sleep (8–10 hr) + nutrition + physical activity + screen time + safety (driving, water, firearms) + substance use + healthy relationships + consent + media literacy + transitions (college/career) + emerging adulthood. INVOLVE the adolescent as primary decision-maker for their health to the extent of their developmental capacity.
labs.r: ADOLESCENT WELL-CARE structure with confidential HEADSSS interview + minor consent navigation is foundational. RN role: facilitate visit format + administer screening tools + provide patient education + coordinate referrals + respect confidentiality + recognize mandatory reporting + imminent danger exceptions. THERAPEUTIC ALLIANCE with adolescent is the foundation — confidentiality builds trust + supports honest disclosure.

labs.n: HEADSSS / HEEADSSS / SSHADESS confidential psychosocial interview
labs.v: HEADSSS (or expanded HEEADSSS or SSHADESS) is the structured psychosocial interview framework for adolescents. ORIGINAL HEADSSS: H = Home; E = Education/Employment; A = Activities; D = Drugs/substance use; S = Sexuality; S = Suicide/mental health; S = Safety. EXPANDED HEEADSSS adds E = Eating Disorders + S = Spirituality + S = Strengths + Safety. SSHADESS reframes opening with Strengths first (positive framing). PRINCIPLES: (1) START with rapport + lower-stakes topics → progress to higher-stakes (sex + suicide + drugs); (2) Open-ended questions + neutral non-judgmental tone; (3) NORMALIZE questions ('Many teens I see deal with...'); (4) Use ADOLESCENT-friendly language; (5) Ask DIRECTLY about sensitive topics (no euphemism — 'thoughts of suicide' not 'thoughts of dark thoughts'); (6) USE structured screening tools (PHQ-9, GAD-7, CRAFFT, SCOFF, C-SSRS, ASQ) — quantify + standardize; (7) RESPECT pace + comfort + return to topics later; (8) DOCUMENT findings + use ICD codes appropriately + safeguard confidentiality in chart sharing. SAMPLE OPENING QUESTIONS: HOME = 'Who lives with you?' 'How are things at home?' 'Anyone you can talk to?'; EDUCATION = 'Tell me about school. How are your grades?' 'Friends?' 'Any bullying?'; ACTIVITIES = 'What do you enjoy?' 'Sports? Music? Art?' 'How much time on phone/social media?'; DRUGS = 'Some teens drink, smoke, or use other substances. Do you?' 'What about your friends?' 'Any concerns?'; SEXUALITY = 'Have you had any relationships?' 'Are you attracted to boys, girls, both, or are you still figuring it out?' 'Have you had sex — including oral, vaginal, anal?' 'Contraception?' 'STI concerns?'; SUICIDE/MENTAL HEALTH = 'How is your mood?' 'Have you ever felt so sad you didn't want to be alive?' 'Have you thought about hurting yourself or ending your life?' 'Have you ever tried?'; SAFETY = 'Seatbelts?' 'Helmets?' 'Driving — do you feel safe in cars?' 'Are there guns in your home?' 'Where are they stored?' 'Have you been hurt by someone?' Always RESPECT the adolescent's voice + autonomy + experiences. ADDRESS strengths + resilience throughout (SSHADESS approach). FOLLOW-UP on disclosures with empathy + validation + appropriate intervention.
labs.r: HEADSSS interview is the structured psychosocial assessment for adolescents. KEY POINTS: (1) confidentiality framed first, (2) open-ended + non-judgmental + direct, (3) structured screening tools quantify, (4) RESPECT pace + autonomy, (5) strengths + resilience addressed, (6) findings documented + acted on. RN role: facilitate setting + administer screening tools + provide patient-centered support + recognize concerning findings + coordinate intervention.

labs.v: ACIP-RECOMMENDED IMMUNIZATIONS for adolescents 11–18 yr: (1) TDAP (tetanus + diphtheria + acellular pertussis) — 1 dose at 11–12 yr; if not done, catch-up at next visit; Td/Tdap booster every 10 yr after age 19; (2) HPV (human papillomavirus) — 9-valent (Gardasil 9) covering HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58; routine at age 11–12 (can start at 9 yr); 2-DOSE series (0, 6–12 mo) if initiated <15 yr; 3-DOSE series (0, 1–2, 6 mo) if initiated ≥15 yr; vaccination prevents oropharyngeal + cervical + anal + penile + vulvar + vaginal cancers + warts; HIGH-VALUE immunization (cancer prevention); (3) MENINGOCOCCAL ACWY (MenACWY) — 1 dose at 11–12 yr; BOOSTER at 16 yr; high-risk groups (HIV, asplenia, complement deficiency, eculizumab, microbiology lab workers, military recruits, travelers to endemic areas) additional doses; (4) MENINGOCOCCAL B (MenB — Bexsero or Trumenba) — Shared Clinical Decision-Making (SCDM) at 16–23 yr (preferred 16–18 yr) for adolescents NOT at increased risk; ROUTINE for high-risk (asplenia, complement deficiency, eculizumab, microbiology lab workers, outbreak); 2-dose series; (5) INFLUENZA annually for all 6 mo+ + during influenza season; (6) COVID-19 per current CDC guidance + age + risk; (7) HEPATITIS B — routine series in infancy + catch-up if not received; (8) MEASLES-MUMPS-RUBELLA (MMR) — series typically complete in childhood + catch-up if not; (9) VARICELLA — 2-dose series typically complete; (10) HEPATITIS A — universal recommendation extended to all children + catch-up; (11) RSV monoclonal not for adolescents (infants + high-risk older adults). MINOR CONSENT for VACCINES: most states require parental consent for adolescent vaccines; some states allow minor consent for HPV + others (CA allows minor consent for HPV + hepatitis B + STI-related vaccinations at 12+ per AB 499 — minors aged 12+ can consent to vaccines for STIs/HPV without parent). VACCINE ADMINISTRATION: COUNSEL parents + adolescent on benefits + risks + side effects; use VIS (Vaccine Information Statement) handout per CDC; obtain consent; document; observe 15 min post-vaccination for syncope/reactions. THIS PATIENT'S IMMUNIZATION STATUS: complete Tdap age 11; HPV #1 + #2 at 11 + 12 = series complete (2-dose initiated <15); MenACWY age 11 (BOOSTER due age 16 = ~1 yr); MenB not yet (SCDM at next visit age 16); influenza annually (last 7 mo ago — due this fall); COVID-19 up to date per current guidance.
labs.r: ACIP IMMUNIZATIONS for adolescents per Bright Futures + Health Promotion. RN role: review immunization records + identify gaps + administer + counsel + document + use VIS handout. CA allows minor consent for STI-related vaccines (including HPV) at 12+ per AB 499. HPV is HIGH-VALUE immunization (cancer prevention). MenACWY booster at 16 yr.

labs.v: PHQ-9 ADOLESCENT (PHQ-9 + or PHQ-A) — 9-item self-report tool for major depressive disorder screening; each item 0–3 (not at all, several days, more than half the days, nearly every day); TOTAL score 0–27; cutoffs: 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, ≥20 severe; ITEM 9 = suicide/self-harm thoughts ('thoughts of being better off dead, or of hurting yourself in some way') — any positive score on item 9 requires FOLLOW-UP risk assessment regardless of total score. THIS PATIENT PHQ-9 = 16/27 = moderately severe depression. C-SSRS (Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale) — semi-structured interview for suicide risk stratification: (1) WISH TO BE DEAD; (2) NON-SPECIFIC ACTIVE SUICIDAL THOUGHTS; (3) ACTIVE IDEATION WITH METHOD (no plan); (4) ACTIVE IDEATION WITH SOME INTENT; (5) ACTIVE IDEATION WITH SPECIFIC PLAN + INTENT; suicidal BEHAVIOR (attempts, aborted, interrupted, preparatory); risk INCREASES with ideation severity + intent + plan + access to means + prior attempts + impulsivity + co-occurring substance use. ASQ (Ask Suicide-Screening Questions) — 4-item screen for pediatric ED/inpatient developed by NIMH: (1) In the past few weeks, have you wished you were dead? (2) In the past few weeks, have you felt that you or your family would be better off if you were dead? (3) In the past week, have you been having thoughts about killing yourself? (4) Have you ever tried to kill yourself? Positive screen = ANY YES → full risk assessment by mental health professional. THIS PATIENT ASQ = #1 YES (wishes she could 'just not exist' = passive), #2 NO, #3 NO, #4 NO = PASSIVE ideation; positive screen; full risk assessment completed. SUICIDE RISK STRATIFICATION for this patient: PASSIVE ideation YES; ACTIVE ideation with method NO; ACTIVE ideation with intent NO; ACTIVE ideation with plan + intent NO; no prior attempts; no self-harm behaviors; ACCESS to means = firearms in home (will be removed today) + medications (will be secured today); PROTECTIVE factors = supportive grandmother + dog + soccer + family engagement + future plans + no substance use + no prior attempts + receptive to help; RISK LEVEL = MODERATE (passive ideation in context of moderate depression with treatable factors + protective factors + family engagement + outpatient mental health intake same day + safety plan); SAFETY PLAN per Stanley-Brown brief intervention; SAME-DAY mental health intake; outpatient therapy + follow-up; LETHAL MEANS RESTRICTION (firearms removed, medications secured); 24/7 crisis lines + ER for active SI/plan/intent emergence. HIGHER risk levels (active ideation + plan + intent + access + prior attempts + impulsivity + substance use co-occurring) may require ED evaluation + psychiatric hospitalization + closer monitoring. ADOLESCENTS have HIGHER risk for impulsive suicide attempts than adults (frontal lobe development continues until ~25 yr — impulse control + future-thinking limited).
labs.r: DEPRESSION + SUICIDE risk assessment + stratification using validated tools (PHQ-9, C-SSRS, ASQ) is essential for adolescent care. POSITIVE PHQ-9 item 9 requires FOLLOW-UP regardless of total score. ASQ is rapid screen; positive → full risk assessment by MH professional. STRATIFICATION guides intervention level (safety plan + outpatient vs ED + inpatient). LETHAL MEANS restriction central to suicide prevention. SAFETY PLAN (Stanley-Brown) is brief evidence-based intervention. ADOLESCENT impulsivity = higher attempt risk than adults.

labs.n: SAFETY PLAN (Stanley-Brown brief intervention) + lethal means restriction
labs.v: STANLEY-BROWN SAFETY PLANNING INTERVENTION — evidence-based brief intervention developed for suicide prevention; reduces re-attempt risk; 6 STEPS personalized with patient: (1) WARNING SIGNS — thoughts, feelings, behaviors, situations that precede suicide crisis (helps patient recognize when at risk + need to use plan; e.g., isolating, ruminating, sleep worse, headaches worsening); (2) INTERNAL COPING STRATEGIES — what patient can do alone to distract + manage (e.g., walk dog, listen to music, exercise, art, journaling, video games); (3) SOCIAL CONTACTS for distraction (not for problem-solving) — people/places to be around (e.g., friend, sibling, grandmother, coffee shop, park); (4) PEOPLE WHO PROVIDE SUPPORT — trusted family/friends who can help (e.g., mom, grandmother, school counselor) with phone numbers; (5) PROFESSIONALS + CRISIS RESOURCES — PCP, therapist, 988 Lifeline, 741741 Crisis Text Line, ED, hospital with names + numbers; (6) MEANS RESTRICTION — make environment safer (lock firearms, secure medications, remove sharp objects, etc.). REVIEW with patient + family + WRITTEN copy patient keeps; UPDATE as needed. LETHAL MEANS RESTRICTION — most effective single intervention; FIREARMS leading method of completed adolescent suicide in US; FIREARM access in home is INDEPENDENT RISK FACTOR for suicide (RR 2–5×); LOCKED + UNLOADED + ammunition stored separately reduces risk; TEMPORARY RELOCATION outside home during crisis (to relative, friend, gun shop, police storage) is most protective; SECURED with trigger lock + cable lock + gun safe; SAME APPROACH for medications (prescription + OTC) + sharp objects + ropes/cords. COUNSELING ON ACCESS TO LETHAL MEANS (CALM) — counseling parents + caregivers; LEGAL TOOLS — Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPO, 'red flag laws') in many states allow temporary removal of firearms when court finds risk. COMMUNICATING with FAMILY = key to lethal means restriction + safety planning success; with adolescent CONSENT for safety info + age-appropriate boundaries (share concerns + plan + means restriction without violating full HEADSSS confidentiality). CRISIS RESOURCES — 988 Suicide + Crisis Lifeline (call/text/chat), Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741), Trevor Project (LGBTQ+ youth — 1-866-488-7386 + text), Trans Lifeline (877-565-8860), local mobile crisis teams, ED.
labs.r: STANLEY-BROWN SAFETY PLAN + LETHAL MEANS RESTRICTION are evidence-based suicide prevention interventions. 6 steps personalized + written + shared with family. LETHAL MEANS RESTRICTION is the most effective single intervention — firearms relocated out of home is most protective. CALM counseling with parents. COMMUNICATING with family with patient consent for safety info + age-appropriate boundaries. RN role: support safety planning + reinforce + connect to crisis resources + advocate for means restriction.

labs.n: ADOLESCENT DEPRESSION + ANXIETY treatment — therapy + medication
labs.v: TREATMENT for adolescent depression + anxiety follows STEPPED CARE approach: (1) MILD = psychoeducation + supportive counseling + parent guidance + close monitoring; (2) MODERATE = COGNITIVE BEHAVIORAL THERAPY (CBT) or INTERPERSONAL THERAPY for ADOLESCENTS (IPT-A) — evidence-based; weekly × 8–16 sessions typical; (3) MODERATELY SEVERE or SEVERE or persistent moderate = COMBINED CBT + ANTIDEPRESSANT. ANTIDEPRESSANT options for adolescent depression: (1) FLUOXETINE (Prozac) — FDA-approved for adolescent MDD ≥8 yr (only SSRI with full FDA approval for pediatric depression); start 10 mg daily → 20 mg after 1 week → titrate as needed; (2) ESCITALOPRAM (Lexapro) — FDA-approved for adolescent MDD 12+ yr; start 5–10 mg daily; (3) SERTRALINE (Zoloft) — FDA-approved for adolescent OCD 6+ yr (off-label for depression); often used off-label for adolescent depression; (4) BUPROPION — off-label; useful for atypical depression with low energy or comorbid ADHD; CONTRAINDICATED in eating disorders + lowers seizure threshold; (5) SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) — second-line; (6) AVOID PAROXETINE in adolescents (highest risk of SI in studies). BLACK-BOX WARNING per FDA — all antidepressants have warning of increased suicidal ideation + behavior in patients <25 yr in first weeks of treatment; risk REAL but UNTREATED depression has higher suicide risk; benefit-risk discussion + family education + frequent follow-up (weekly × 4 wk → biweekly × 4 wk → monthly) + crisis plan during initiation. CBT FOR ADOLESCENTS — cognitive restructuring + behavioral activation + problem-solving + emotion regulation + relapse prevention; family involvement varies; manualized programs available. IPT-A — focused on interpersonal relationships + role transitions + role disputes + interpersonal deficits + grief. DBT (dialectical behavior therapy) — for chronic suicidal ideation + self-harm + emotion dysregulation. FAMILY THERAPY — useful for family-system contributors. EXERCISE + SLEEP + nutrition + screen time + sunlight = adjunctive lifestyle interventions. THIS PATIENT plan: CBT initiated through outpatient mental health intake today; weekly × 8 weeks; ANTIDEPRESSANT discussion deferred pending therapy response + further evaluation + family preference; if response inadequate at 6–8 weeks or symptom progression — discuss SSRI initiation (likely fluoxetine 10 mg daily) with informed consent + black-box warning education + frequent follow-up; LIFESTYLE INTERVENTIONS started today — sleep hygiene, nutrition, physical activity (gentle), screen time limits, soccer continuation as appropriate per mental health team.
labs.r: TREATMENT for adolescent depression is STEPPED CARE — CBT first-line for moderate; combined CBT + SSRI for moderately severe + severe; FLUOXETINE most evidence-based + FDA-approved. BLACK-BOX warning on increased SI in <25 yr requires informed consent + family education + frequent follow-up + crisis plan. Lifestyle interventions adjunctive.

labs.n: SPORTS PHYSICAL clearance — PPE-5 standard for soccer participation
labs.v: PRE-PARTICIPATION PHYSICAL EVALUATION (PPE-5) — AAFP/AAP/ACSM/AMSSM/AOSSM/NATA joint guideline for sports physical clearance, updated 2019 (5th edition). PURPOSE: identify conditions that may limit safe participation; screen for sudden cardiac arrest (SCA) risk + other catastrophic risks. COMPONENTS: (1) HISTORY — comprehensive medical + cardiac (chest pain, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue with exertion, family hx SCA <50 yr or HCM/long-QT/Marfan/cardiomyopathy/arrhythmogenic RV cardiomyopathy/Brugada); (2) MUSCULOSKELETAL exam; (3) CARDIOVASCULAR exam (BP, pulses, murmur listening); (4) GENERAL exam; (5) FEMALE ATHLETE TRIAD / RED-S (Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport) — low energy availability + menstrual dysfunction + low BMD; weight loss + amenorrhea + stress fractures; this patient's 6-mo weight loss + nutritional decline warrants attention; (6) CONCUSSION history + baseline; (7) ERGOGENIC substances + nutrition + hydration; (8) MENTAL HEALTH; (9) SKIN exam for infectious lesions in contact sports. CLEARANCE outcomes: CLEARED, CLEARED with recommendations, CLEARED after evaluation, NOT CLEARED for specific sport. SCA risk factors warrant 12-lead ECG screening per some recommendations (universal vs targeted is debated — AHA recommends targeted history-based screening + ECG only for symptomatic or family hx; some advocate universal ECG screening); echocardiogram for positive findings. SUDDEN CARDIAC ARREST in young athletes — leading cause = HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY (HCM), CORONARY ARTERY ANOMALIES, LONG-QT, MYOCARDITIS, COMMOTIO CORDIS (impact to chest during precordial vulnerable phase). EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN at every school + AED at every facility + CPR-trained personnel. CONCUSSION management — REMOVE from play same day + medical evaluation + GRADUAL return to play (RTP) protocol + symptom-based progression + cleared by qualified provider; SCAT-6 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool) standardized assessment. THIS PATIENT'S sports clearance: HISTORY mostly reassuring (no cardiac sx, no family hx SCA, no concussion hx, normal exam, no MSK injuries, current BP/HR/rhythm normal); however MENTAL HEALTH concerns + weight loss + decreased appetite warrant ATTENTION; clearance HELD pending: (1) stability of mental health, (2) clearance by LCSW + PCP after 2-week stabilization period, (3) nutrition + weight stabilization, (4) gradual progressive activity (continuing soccer training appropriate if cleared by MH team for emotional benefit + structure + social support); FORMAL clearance form completed pending these elements.
labs.r: SPORTS PHYSICAL clearance per PPE-5 standard — comprehensive history + exam + SCA risk + mental health + female athlete triad. CLEARANCE outcomes vary. THIS PATIENT clearance HELD pending mental health stability + LCSW clearance. RN role: gather PPE-5 history + screening + coordinate clearance + provide patient education on activity benefits + safety.

labs.n: ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE + lifestyle interventions for adolescents
labs.v: ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE topics for adolescent well-care per Bright Futures: (1) SLEEP — recommend 8–10 hr/night for teens 13–18 yr; sleep hygiene (consistent schedule, dark room, no screens 1 hr before bed, no caffeine after noon, no alcohol/marijuana, no late-night intense exercise); this patient currently ~5.5 hr/night — significant impact on mood + cognition + immune + metabolic health; school start times often misaligned with adolescent circadian rhythm (~11 pm sleep onset natural; pushing earlier is challenging); (2) NUTRITION — balanced + Mediterranean-style with vegetables + fruits + whole grains + lean protein + healthy fats; iron-rich foods (especially menstruating females); calcium + vitamin D for bone health; limit added sugars + ultra-processed foods + sugar-sweetened beverages; this patient with decreased appetite + 3 kg loss → SCOFF screen done (negative for eating disorder) + nutrition consult + family meal patterns; (3) PHYSICAL ACTIVITY — 60 min/day moderate-to-vigorous activity for ages 6–17; bone-strengthening + muscle-strengthening 3×/wk; soccer fits well; gentle progression appropriate during emotional recovery; (4) SCREEN TIME — limit recreational screen time + 1 hr before bed + remove from bedroom at night; current 5+ hr/day potentially detrimental for mental health (social comparison + reduced sleep + reduced physical activity + reduced face-to-face interaction); negotiate reasonable limits + media literacy + healthy relationships with technology; CYBERBULLYING + sextortion + online predators awareness; (5) SAFETY — seatbelts every ride + helmets (bike, ski, skateboard) + no texting/calling while driving + GDL (graduated driver licensing) compliance + alcohol/drug-free driving + designated driver + bystander training; (6) SUBSTANCE USE — discussions about peer pressure + refusal skills + harm reduction + delaying first use (later first use → lower lifetime risk); (7) HEALTHY RELATIONSHIPS + CONSENT — discussions about boundaries + consent + healthy vs unhealthy relationships + intimate partner violence + sexual coercion + sexting + LGBTQ-affirming care; (8) MENTAL HEALTH + STRESS MANAGEMENT — coping skills + mindfulness + journaling + talking + when to seek help; (9) IDENTITY + AUTONOMY — affirm developing identity + support exploration; (10) FUTURE PLANNING — academic + career + transitional needs; (11) DENTAL — twice-yearly cleanings + fluoride + flossing; (12) SUN PROTECTION + skin self-exam; (13) ORAL contraception/contraception/STI prevention if sexually active; (14) HEARING + vision + dental + lipid + HIV + chlamydia/gonorrhea + cervical cancer screening per guidelines.
labs.r: ANTICIPATORY GUIDANCE for adolescents is comprehensive — sleep + nutrition + activity + screen time + safety + substance use + relationships + mental health + identity + future. Tailored to individual + developmental stage + risk factors. RN role: deliver guidance during well-care + at every encounter + use motivational interviewing + adolescent-friendly approach + handouts + resources.

labs.n: CONFIDENTIALITY + PARENT engagement balance — age-appropriate disclosure
labs.v: CONFIDENTIALITY-PARENT BALANCE is one of the most challenging + nuanced aspects of adolescent care. PRINCIPLES: (1) ESTABLISH CONFIDENTIALITY FRAMEWORK at every visit + explain limits + reaffirm; (2) MINOR CONSENT laws (CA Family Code §§6924-6929) protect adolescents' access to mental health + reproductive health + STI + substance use services without parental notification; (3) ENGAGE parents as partners with patient consent — parent involvement improves outcomes when navigated thoughtfully; (4) EXCEPTIONS to confidentiality clearly communicated + invoked when warranted — mandated reporting (child abuse/neglect — RN mandated reporter all 50 states per CANRA in CA), IMMINENT DANGER (suicide ideation with plan/intent/access OR homicide ideation), court orders; (5) WITH PATIENT CONSENT for partial disclosure — share specific safety concerns + plan + means restriction WITHOUT violating full HEADSSS confidentiality; e.g., 'Your daughter has been screening positive for moderate depression including some thoughts about not wanting to exist; she does not have a specific plan but we are concerned and recommending mental health intake today. I will share the safety plan with you both. The details of what we discussed are between her and me but we agree the safety information is for both of you'; (6) RESPECT the adolescent's voice in deciding what to share + when + how; (7) IF ADOLESCENT REFUSES disclosure when safety concern present — RN can override only in IMMINENT DANGER situations; otherwise work with patient to build comfort + share appropriately; engage adolescent in NEGOTIATING what they want shared + what is left to them to share later; (8) DOCUMENT confidentiality framework + disclosures + reasons + patient response; (9) CULTURAL + family dynamics + immigration status + LGBTQ identity context — additional considerations for confidentiality + disclosure; (10) ONGOING — confidentiality may evolve as patient becomes ready to share more or less. THIS PATIENT: patient CONSENTED to disclosure of safety concerns + plan + means restriction with mother; full HEADSSS details (boyfriend breakup + bullying + specific identity exploration + family conflict + previous mild self-criticism) NOT shared per patient request — appropriate age-aligned boundary; mother briefed by RN + PCP on safety concerns + plan + means restriction + mental health intake same day + crisis resources + family supports; FAMILY ENGAGED + supportive + grateful. INSTITUTIONAL ELECTRONIC HEALTH RECORD considerations — sensitive content (mental health, reproductive health, substance use, STI) often separated from parent's portal access per institutional policy + state regs; billing privacy similarly.
labs.r: CONFIDENTIALITY-PARENT BALANCE is the foundation of effective adolescent care. ESTABLISH framework + limits + minor consent + exception thresholds. ENGAGE parents as partners with patient consent. AGE-APPROPRIATE disclosure for safety. RESPECT adolescent voice. RN role: facilitate balance + document + advocate for both privacy + safety + family connection.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: NO active suicidal ideation with plan or intent (only passive 'better off not existing'); NO prior suicide attempts; NO self-harm cutting/burning/other behaviors; NO substance use (CRAFFT 0/6); NO sexual activity to date (deferred contraception); NO eating disorder (SCOFF negative); NO concussion history; NO chronic medical conditions; NO acute physical findings; NO firearms or medications immediately accessible to patient (in process of removing/securing today); NO active homicidal ideation; NO active psychosis; NO acute trauma or recent abuse exposure; immunizations up to date (Tdap + HPV completed; MenACWY due booster age 16 ~1 yr out; influenza + COVID up to date; MenB SCDM at next visit); growth + development on trajectory (mild weight loss attributable to depression — addressed); academic skills present (drop is recent + reversible); no learning disability or major behavioral health diagnosis prior; insurance coverage active (parent's employer plan + Medi-Cal as supplement). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: SUPPORTIVE GRANDMOTHER + half-brother + family dog; SOCCER team participation + identity + structure; future plans (college, career interests); receptive to therapy + mental health intake; family engagement + cooperation with safety plan; no history of substance use; no prior attempts; access to immediate mental health services (same-day intake); insurance coverage; PCP medical home with continuity; school counselor available; coping skills + interests (art, music, walking dog) identified + can be rebuilt; relatively brief duration of symptoms (~2–3 months); no severe depression + no major risk factors; Buddhist spiritual heritage providing meaning + grandmother connection; trauma-informed + adolescent-friendly clinic environment + experienced team.
labs.r: Multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support good outcome. Passive ideation + no plan/intent/prior attempts + family engagement + same-day MH intake + safety plan + means restriction + outpatient therapy + lifestyle interventions + strengths-based approach = good prognosis. STANDARD ADOLESCENT depression with appropriate intervention shows ~50–70% improvement with CBT + ~50–60% with SSRI alone + ~70–80% with combined therapy.

nursesNote: 1355 — 15 y/o F A. Tanaka roomed by MA for scheduled annual well-care + sports physical clearance for soccer; mother present (52 y/o pharmacist; supportive engaged co-decision-maker per chart). Visit format: joint with mother 10 min → CONFIDENTIAL HEADSSS interview alone 20 min → joint summary + plan 15 min. PRE-VISIT vitals: HR 78, BP 108/64 (50–75th percentile age + height = normotensive per AAP 2017 BP table), RR 14, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.7°C, ht 162 cm (50–75th %ile), wt 51 kg (10–25th %ile; DOWN 3 kg from 6 mo ago — clinically significant weight loss in adolescent warranting attention), BMI 19.4 (25th %ile; healthy range 5th–85th %ile), vision 20/20 bilateral via Snellen, hearing screen pass, dental last cleaning 4 mo ago. JOINT PORTION with mother 1405: mother reports 'mostly here for sports physical and annual checkup; she's been doing fine but seems tired and quieter lately.' On RN intake interview = SOMATIC complaints from patient = chronic intermittent tension-type HA × 2 mo (2–3/wk, no aura, no neuro deficits, no early morning HA, no vomiting), fatigue × 2 mo, occasional epigastric discomfort × 6 wk, decreased appetite × 2 mo, sleep onset insomnia × 6 wk (in bed 2230, falls asleep 0030–0100, wakes 0630 for school = ~5.5 hr / night vs AAP-recommended 8–10 hr for age — significant sleep debt); academic performance dropped from A to B−/C+ this semester per mother (patient acknowledges); SOCCER varsity team try-outs in 2 wk; menarche age 12 + regular cycles q28d / 5-day flow / LMP 18 d ago; no pregnancy possibility per patient (deferred to confidential portion). CONFIDENTIALITY framed at 1420 by PCP + RN: 'What you tell us is between you and us unless you tell us you're in immediate danger of hurting yourself or others, OR someone is hurting you, OR we're required by law to report child abuse/neglect. Otherwise this stays between us.' MOTHER stepped out for confidential HEADSSS portion at 1422. HEADSSS CONFIDENTIAL INTERVIEW 1422–1442: HOME = lives with mom + stepfather (married 3 yr; now strained — 'we don't really get along anymore'); biological father absent since age 8 (parents divorced); 9 y/o half-brother (close protective relationship); MATERNAL GRANDMOTHER close + 'my person'; recent move 4 mo ago to new house ~30 min from prior; home generally safe; no domestic violence; FIREARMS in home (stepfather hunter; 2 long guns in safe upstairs; patient does NOT know combination); MEDICATIONS = mother's prescription antidepressant in UNLOCKED kitchen drawer (will discuss + secure). EDUCATION = sophomore at large public high school; A student → B−/C+ this semester (drop noted by parents + teachers); some BULLYING at new school by 'mean girls' clique (verbal + social exclusion + social media); school nurse + counselor available + patient unaware of services. ACTIVITIES = soccer + USED to enjoy art + reading but has STOPPED both ('I just don't feel like it anymore'); 5+ hr/day phone/social media (TikTok + Instagram + Snapchat) with negative emotional impact + comparison; no regular friends at new school (lost touch with old friends after move). DRUGS = CRAFFT 0/6 = no high-risk substance use; no current alcohol/tobacco/vaping/cannabis/other; aware peers use; not using. SEXUALITY = cisgender female + heterosexual; 1 boyfriend × 3 mo (broke up 5 wk ago — 'he was getting controlling + wanted to do more than I wanted'); never sexually active (no oral/vaginal/anal intercourse to date); contraception offered (declined currently — abstinent); STI testing offered (declined — not currently active + no concerns); HPV/Tdap complete at 11–12 yr (charted); MenACWY done age 11 + booster due age 16 ~1 yr; MenB SCDM at next visit. SUICIDE/MENTAL HEALTH = PHQ-9 administered = 16/27 (moderately severe depression — cutoffs 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, ≥20 severe); positive items = depressed mood + decreased interest + sleep + fatigue + appetite + worthlessness + concentration + psychomotor + ITEM 9 'thoughts of being better off dead or hurting yourself' = score 1 'several days' — followed up with Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) + ASQ: ASQ #1 'in past few weeks have you wished you were dead?' = YES (sometimes wishes she could 'just not exist'); ASQ #2 'felt you/family would be better off if you were dead?' = NO; ASQ #3 'in past week thoughts about killing yourself?' = NO; ASQ #4 'ever tried to kill yourself?' = NO; C-SSRS = PASSIVE ideation YES + ACTIVE ideation with method NO + ACTIVE ideation with intent NO + ACTIVE ideation with plan + intent NO + no prior attempts + no self-harm. GAD-7 = 12/21 moderate anxiety. SCOFF eating disorder screen = 0/5 NEGATIVE (weight loss attributed to depression-related decreased appetite + mild body image concerns not driving behavior). SAFETY = always wears seatbelt + bike helmet; school bus rider; no concussion history; concussion training annually with soccer team; FIREARMS as above; MEDICATION as above; pool fenced; smoke detectors functioning. SPIRITUALITY = Buddhist heritage + occasional temple with grandmother + meaning + grounding in grandmother's wisdom; not currently major conflict or support. SUICIDE RISK STRATIFICATION: passive ideation + no plan/intent/access/prior attempts + protective factors (grandmother + soccer + dog + family + future plans + receptive to help + same-day MH intake + family cooperative with means restriction) = MODERATE RISK. PHYSICAL EXAM (PPE-5 sports clearance): CARDIAC — no chest pain, no syncope, no exertional dyspnea, no palpitations, no family hx SCA; exam — HR 84 NSR, S1/S2 normal, no murmurs/rubs/gallops, peripheral pulses 2+ symmetric, BP 108/64; PULMONARY — clear to auscultation bilateral, no wheeze/rhonchi; MSK — no joint deformity, full ROM, no MSK injuries; SKIN — no concerning lesions; ABDOMINAL — soft non-tender; female athlete triad / RED-S screen — regular menses + no stress fractures + weight loss attributed to depression not low energy availability primary; CONCUSSION baseline assessment + no history; clearance HELD pending mental health stability + LCSW clearance (recommendation = continue gentle soccer training for emotional/social benefit + structure if cleared by MH team; formal sports clearance form pending). PATIENT CONSENTED at end of confidential portion to disclosure with mother of: (1) safety concerns + plan, (2) means restriction recommendations, (3) same-day MH intake referral, (4) general statement about depression + anxiety symptoms — but NOT full HEADSSS details (boyfriend + bullying + family conflict + identity nuances) — appropriate age-aligned boundary respected. JOINT PORTION with mother 1440: PCP + RN summarized to mother (with patient present + consenting): screening tools (without disclosing scores) revealed symptoms consistent with moderate depression + anxiety including some passive thoughts about not existing without specific plan or intent; recommendation = SAME-DAY mental health intake (LCSW at same medical center within 3 hr) + outpatient CBT-based therapy + further evaluation + FIREARM SAFETY (lock guns at temporary off-site storage with stepfather's brother or trigger locks + remove keys + ammunition stored separately) + MEDICATION SECURING (lock or remove from home — mother's antidepressants relocated to bedroom locked cabinet) + family + sleep + nutrition + screen time + social support; SPORTS CLEARANCE pending stability + LCSW clearance (continued gentle training appropriate if MH-team-approved). Mother visibly distressed but engaged + cooperative + grateful — 'I knew something was wrong but I didn't know how to ask'; committed to firearm + medication safety same day (will ask stepfather to take guns to her brother's safe for 30+ days); family meeting scheduled with MH intake provider 1700 today; patient + mother agree to safety plan + follow-up. CRISIS RESOURCES provided + WRITTEN copies: 988 Suicide + Crisis Lifeline (call/text/chat), Crisis Text Line (text HOME to 741741), local mobile crisis 1-800-XXX-XXXX, ED if active SI/plan/intent emergence; PROVIDED handout on adolescent depression + warning signs + means restriction + sleep hygiene + screen time + nutrition; SAFETY PLAN (Stanley-Brown) co-created during MH intake later 1700. IMMUNIZATIONS reviewed: HPV complete (2-dose initiated <15 yr); Tdap done; MenACWY done 11; MenB SCDM next visit; INFLUENZA last 7 mo ago + due this fall (offered today + accepted) — administered influenza quadrivalent 0.5 mL IM deltoid R + VIS provided + 15-min observation post-vaccination without reaction; COVID-19 up to date per current guidance. ADDITIONAL preventive: HBV/HAV/MMR/VAR series complete childhood. LIFESTYLE INTERVENTIONS initiated today + reinforced: SLEEP HYGIENE (consistent schedule, dark room, no screens 1 hr before bed, no caffeine after noon, no late-night intense activity — bed by 2200, wake 0700 = 9 hr); NUTRITION (3 balanced meals + snacks; nutrition consult referred; family meal patterns; favorite foods reintroduced; calcium + iron + vitamin D); PHYSICAL ACTIVITY (60 min/day moderate-vigorous; continue gentle soccer training pending MH team clearance); SCREEN TIME (limit recreational + 1 hr before bed + remove from bedroom + media literacy + healthy relationships with tech); HEALTHY RELATIONSHIPS + consent education; SUBSTANCE USE harm reduction; SAFETY (seatbelts + helmets + driving + alcohol/drug-free + means restriction). FOLLOW-UP: outpatient mental health 1 week (LCSW therapy) + PCP follow-up 2 weeks for safety re-check + therapy progress + sports clearance reassessment + monthly until stable; SCHOOL counselor coordination via signed release of information (patient + mother signed); family supports + grandmother encouraged to remain involved; consideration of antidepressant (likely fluoxetine 10 mg daily — only FDA-approved SSRI for adolescent depression ≥8 yr; BLACK-BOX warning for increased SI in <25 yr requires informed consent + frequent follow-up + crisis plan) DEFERRED pending therapy response at 6–8 weeks + family/patient preference + symptom trajectory. POST-VISIT documentation: PHQ-9 + GAD-7 + C-SSRS + ASQ scores documented in sensitive mental health section per institutional EHR (separated from parent portal); safety plan attached; LCSW intake note pending; sports clearance form 'HELD pending mental health clearance'; consent for treatment + release of information for school counselor + signed by patient + mother; insurance billing for routine well-care + sports physical + mental health screening + brief intervention coded appropriately; CDPH child abuse/neglect mandatory reporting NOT triggered (no abuse/neglect disclosed); mandatory IMMINENT DANGER threshold NOT crossed (no active plan/intent — appropriate for outpatient management); ER protocol available if active SI/plan/intent emerges before next visit. TEAM debriefing + patient + family experience reviewed; chaplain availability + community resources offered.

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this adolescent well-care + sports physical visit?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: PHQ-9 ADOLESCENT = 16/27 MODERATELY SEVERE depression with positive ITEM 9 (thoughts of being better off dead or hurting yourself score 1 'several days'); GAD-7 = 12/21 moderate anxiety; ASQ positive (#1 YES, #2-4 NO) — full risk assessment with C-SSRS confirms passive ideation without plan/intent/access/prior attempts; CRAFFT 0/6 + SCOFF 0/5 (rule out high-risk substance + eating disorder)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — depression + anxiety with passive suicidal ideation. Validated screening tools quantify severity. POSITIVE PHQ-9 item 9 requires follow-up regardless of total score. C-SSRS + ASQ confirm risk stratification = MODERATE risk. Standard quantitative + qualitative assessment guides intervention level.

opts.text: RISK FACTORS for adolescent depression + suicide: 6-mo weight loss 3 kg (clinically significant), academic drop A → B−/C+ this semester, sleep onset insomnia × 6 wk (~5.5 hr/night vs 8–10 recommended), recent move 4 mo ago (loss of friends), bullying at new school, boyfriend breakup 5 wk ago, stopped enjoying art + reading (anhedonia), 5+ hr/day social media with negative impact, family conflict with stepfather, FIREARMS in home + access to mother's medications in unlocked drawer
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk factor constellation. Multiple modifiable risk factors. ANHEDONIA + somatic symptoms + sleep + appetite + academic + social isolation = depression syndrome. LETHAL MEANS access (firearms + medications) is a critical risk factor for completed suicide — means restriction is most effective single intervention.

opts.text: PROTECTIVE FACTORS: supportive maternal grandmother ('my person'), 9-year-old half-brother (close + protective), family dog, soccer team participation, identifies as cisgender female + heterosexual (low identity-related minority stress in this context), no prior suicide attempts, no self-harm behaviors, no current substance use, no active plan/intent/access ongoing (means restriction in progress), receptive to therapy + MH intake, family engaged + cooperative, insurance coverage, PCP medical home with continuity, Buddhist spiritual heritage, brief symptom duration (~2–3 months)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — protective factors guide risk stratification + intervention level. Multiple protective factors balance risk factors. Family engagement + same-day MH intake + means restriction + outpatient therapy = MODERATE risk appropriate for outpatient management. Strengths-based assessment essential for adolescent care.

opts.text: CONFIDENTIALITY framework: established with patient + caregiver at visit start; minor consent rights (CA Family Code §§6924-6929 mental health + reproductive + STI + substance use at 12+); limits = mandated reporting (child abuse/neglect) + IMMINENT DANGER (active SI with plan/intent/access) + court orders; CONFIDENTIAL HEADSSS portion with mother out of room ~20 min; documentation in sensitive section of EHR separated from parent portal per minor consent + privacy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confidentiality is foundational to adolescent care + builds therapeutic alliance + supports honest disclosure. Minor consent laws + exceptions (mandated reporting + imminent danger). CONFIDENTIAL portion essential + structured. Documentation per institutional minor-consent privacy protocol.

opts.text: SAFETY PLAN (Stanley-Brown brief intervention) + LETHAL MEANS RESTRICTION: 6-step plan with warning signs + internal coping + social distractions + people for support + professionals + means restriction (firearms relocated to off-site safe + medications secured); SAME-DAY MH intake (LCSW within 3 hr of PCP referral); CBT outpatient therapy weekly × initial 8 sessions; antidepressant deferred pending therapy response; 988 + 741741 + ER crisis resources

opts.text: SPORTS PHYSICAL CLEARANCE per PPE-5: comprehensive history + exam + SCA risk + female athlete triad/RED-S + concussion + mental health + ergogenic substances; CLEARED, CLEARED with recommendations, CLEARED after evaluation, NOT CLEARED outcomes; SCA leading causes = HCM + coronary anomalies + long-QT + myocarditis + commotio cordis; EAP + AED at every facility; concussion management = REMOVE + evaluate + GRADUAL RTP

opts.text: Confidentiality of HEADSSS interview is ABSOLUTE — RN should never share any of the content with mother under any circumstance, even if patient discloses imminent suicide plan with intent + means
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. CONFIDENTIALITY has LIMITS clearly explained at outset: (1) mandated reporting (child abuse/neglect), (2) IMMINENT DANGER to self or others (active SI with plan/intent/access OR homicide ideation), (3) court orders. IMMINENT DANGER (suicide plan + intent + means) IS A CONFIDENTIALITY EXCEPTION + RN/PCP can + should share with caregiver + activate emergency services. Adolescent's confidentiality is supported BUT NOT at the cost of their safety. Communicating limits at outset + honoring exceptions when triggered.

opts.text: All adolescents who score positive on PHQ-9 item 9 OR ASQ should be admitted to a psychiatric facility for evaluation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. RISK STRATIFICATION guides intervention. PASSIVE ideation + no plan/intent/access + protective factors + family engagement + outpatient MH intake + safety plan + means restriction = OUTPATIENT management appropriate (this patient's situation). ACTIVE ideation with plan/intent/access OR prior attempts OR inability to maintain safety OR severe symptoms = ED + possible inpatient. Over-hospitalization can be traumatic + counterproductive + miss therapeutic alliance + family engagement opportunity. Use validated risk stratification not blanket admission.

opts.text: Black-box warning means SSRIs are CONTRAINDICATED in adolescents under 25 — they should NEVER be prescribed because they cause suicide
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG interpretation. UNTREATED severe depression has HIGHER suicide risk than treated depression with appropriate SSRI + monitoring. FLUOXETINE is FDA-APPROVED for adolescent MDD ≥8 yr (only fully approved SSRI for pediatric depression) precisely because benefits outweigh risks for moderately severe + severe cases. Stepped care approach: CBT first-line for moderate; combined CBT + SSRI for moderately severe + severe.
